content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You meet it on your very first day. println!("Hello, world!"). That little exclamation mark, sitting politely after the word println. Most newcomers file it away as "the thing Rust makes me type for printing" and move on.
But that one keystroke is doing more work than almost any other character in the language. Depending on where you put it, ! is three completely different things — and the third one quietly holds up a corner of Rust's type system that most people never notice.
Let’s pull the thread.
Hat #1: The Macro Marker
The ! you already know. When you write println!, vec!, format!, or assert!, the ! is telling the compiler: this is a macro, not a function.
let v = vec![1, 2, 3]; // macro: builds a Vec
let s = format!("{}-{}", 1, 2); // macro: builds a String
println!("hello"); // macro: prints to stdoutWhy mark them at all? Because macros don’t play by function rules. They run at compile time, they can take a variable number of arguments, and they can expand into arbitrary code. vec![1, 2, 3] isn't a call — it becomes a block that allocates a vector and pushes three elements. The ! is a heads-up to both the compiler and to you: "something more than a function call is happening here."
